note_data.title: Information representation
keyPoints: **Binary** uses base 2 with digits 0 and 1 only, representing all data in computers using electrical states
**Hexadecimal** uses base 16 with digits 0-9 and A-F, providing a shorter representation of binary values
**Two's complement** represents signed integers by inverting all bits and adding 1 to get the negative value
**BCD (Binary Coded Decimal)** represents each decimal digit using 4 bits, making conversion between decimal and binary easier
**ASCII** uses 7 bits to represent 128 characters including letters, numbers and symbols used in English
**Unicode** uses 16 bits or more to represent characters from all world languages including Urdu characters
Each hexadecimal digit represents exactly 4 binary digits, making conversion straightforward
Most significant bit in two's complement indicates the sign (0 = positive, 1 = negative)
BCD uses more storage space than pure binary but eliminates rounding errors in decimal calculations

definitions.definition: A number system using base 2 with only two digits (0 and 1) to represent all values
A number system using base 16 with digits 0-9 and letters A-F representing values 10-15
A method of representing signed binary numbers where negative numbers are formed by inverting all bits and adding 1
American Standard Code for Information Interchange - a 7-bit character encoding standard for representing text characters
A character encoding standard that uses 16 bits or more to represent characters from all world writing systems

howToAnswer.instruction: Show each step of the conversion process. For binary to hex, group into 4s. For two's complement, show invert then add 1.
Give the reason why that representation is used, e.g. 'because it eliminates rounding errors' or 'because it can represent more characters'
Give the fact directly without explanation, e.g. 'ASCII uses 7 bits'

modelAnswer.question: Explain why Unicode is used instead of ASCII for representing text in Pakistani software applications. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Unicode can represent characters from multiple languages including Urdu characters which ASCII cannot represent. ASCII only uses 7 bits allowing 128 characters which is insufficient for non-English languages. Unicode uses 16 bits or more allowing representation of characters from all world writing systems including Arabic script used in Urdu.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Unicode can represent Urdu/non-English characters
Mark 2: ASCII limited to 128 characters/English only
Mark 3: Unicode uses more bits allowing more character representations

commonMistakes: Never say 'ASCII uses 8 bits' — always say 'ASCII uses 7 bits'
Students often forget to add 1 when calculating two's complement after inverting bits
Don't confuse BCD with binary — BCD uses 4 bits per decimal digit not the most efficient binary representation
Never say 'hexadecimal is easier for humans' — say 'hexadecimal provides a more compact representation of binary'
Students often give the one's complement (just inverted bits) instead of two's complement
examinerTips: Number base conversions appear in every Paper 2 exam session
Always show working for conversions even if not explicitly asked
Remember that F in hexadecimal equals 15 in decimal and 1111 in binary
Two's complement questions often ask for both positive and negative representations
formulas: Hex to decimal: Multiply each digit by 16^position
Binary to decimal: Multiply each bit by 2^position
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Place value tables showing powers of 2 for binary, powers of 16 for hexadecimal, and bit patterns for two's complement showing sign bit position
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q2 (6 marks) — they asked students to convert the denary number 156 to binary and hexadecimal, then explain why hexadecimal is used as shorthand for binary
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q1 (4 marks) — students had to identify overflow in 8-bit two's complement addition and explain what causes it
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — candidates needed to convert negative denary numbers to two's complement binary and perform subtraction using addition
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q4 (3 marks) — students were asked to explain the difference between analogue and digital data with examples of each

workedExample.question: Convert the denary number -19 to 8-bit two's complement binary. Show all working. (4 marks)
steps: Step 2: Find one's complement by inverting all bits: 11101100
Step 3: Add 1 to get two's complement: 11101100 + 1 = 11101101
Step 4: Verify by checking sign bit is 1 (negative) and range is within -128 to +127
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for converting +19 to binary correctly, 1 mark for one's complement, 1 mark for adding 1, 1 mark for final correct answer

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused one's complement with two's complement' — always remember two's complement requires inverting bits AND adding 1
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often forgot to show the most significant bit when converting to binary' — always pad to the specified number of bits (usually 8 or 16)
Examiner Report 2023: 'Common error was not recognizing overflow had occurred in binary arithmetic' — check if result exceeds the range possible with given number of bits
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a number base conversion table showing denary (0-15), binary (0000-1111), and hexadecimal (0-F) equivalents. Include column headers clearly labeled and show the relationship between 4 binary digits equaling 1 hexadecimal digit. Also draw a two's complement range diagram showing -128 to +127 for 8-bit with the binary representations of key values like -1 (11111111) and -128 (10000000).
note_data.quickRecap: Information is represented in binary (base 2) using only 0s and 1s. Positive integers use standard binary, negative integers use two's complement (invert bits + add 1). Hexadecimal is base 16 shorthand where each hex digit represents 4 binary digits. Always check for overflow when the result exceeds the possible range for the given number of bits.
